summary: SOTA court detection/tracking/calibration research for single-camera racket sports
logs: Budget: ~30 sonnet agents (6 scouts M, ~12 deep-reads M, 1 critic H, <=6 gap-fill M, <=20 refute votes H capped by claim cap, 1 synth H)

key_facts: ITF's Hawk-Eye lab-test ground truth (Dec 2004) used a Phantom high-speed camera at 2000Hz/512x512px over a calibrated 0.5x0.5m FOV (~1mm/px), with ITF's own reference reading uncertainty of ±0.9mm — a physics-metrology rig, not an ML-labeled dataset (itftennis.com/media/12242/line-calling.pdf).

Hawk-Eye Officiating (ITF-certified) is an 8-10 fixed-camera, 50/60Hz rig, each camera on its own PC, all on a wired isolated network feeding one control PC that fuses multi-view images against 'a virtual model of the court' — confirmed multi-view, not monocular (itftennis.com/media/6216, PAT-13-002 report, tested 6 Dec 2013).

Hawk-Eye Officiating was ITF-approved 14 Oct 2005 and first used at Hopman Cup Jan 2006; Hawk-Eye Live (real-time) was not certified until June 2020 — a 14-year gap between offline-replay and live-call approval.

As of Dec 2023 only 5 systems had passed ITF's line-calling evaluation: Hawk-Eye/Hawk-Eye Live, FOXTENN/FOXTENN Live, IMG Arena(FlightScope)/IMG Arena Live, and Bolt6 Sentinel; none of these regulatory documents disclose any algorithm, camera-calibration method, or license.

SwingVision's own patent (US11893808B2, filed Nov 2020, Mangolytics Inc) describes a single 2D phone camera feeding a neural net trained to correlate object pixels (ball/player) with 'regulation topology' (known court-line geometry) to regress 3D properties end-to-end, not via an explicit homography/keypoint-solver pipeline — trained against ground truth from co-located radar/Lidar rigs at multiple physically instrumented events, not from labeled frames alone; no accuracy numbers, no architecture, no homography method disclosed.

pb.vision (our reference diagnostic) requires a single STATIC camera (tripod/fence-mount, ≥5ft height, no handheld, no camera cuts) with all 4 court corners visible for the whole clip, gated by a pre-recording 'CourtFocus' lock-on UI, and explicitly rejects tournament/multi-angle footage — their production design pushes viewpoint-robustness burden onto capture UX/constrained input rather than solving arbitrary-camera-motion court tracking in the model (help.pb.vision).

FOXTENN's in/out method is architecturally different from Hawk-Eye: ground-level synchronized high-speed cameras + laser scanners imaging the ball's actual court contact mark directly at up to 2500fps, rather than reconstructing/estimating trajectory from elevated multi-view cameras.
Zenniz fuses vision with acoustics: patented 'Dual Tracking' = 4 cameras + a 30-microphone sound-triangulation array analyzing bounce impact acoustic signatures.

TVCalib (WACV 2023, MIT license, github.com/mm4spa/tvcalib) reframes field registration as full 3D camera-parameter optimization via a differentiable segment-reprojection loss over line/point-cloud correspondences — the calibration/optimization step itself needs ZERO training data (only the upstream segmentation net does); homography-decompose-then-refine (structurally like our no-op step) is measurably worse than direct reprojection-loss optimization in their own ablation (AC@5 48.1% vs 68.7% GT-segments, SN-Calib-test-center).

TVCalib's own best numbers cap out at AC@5px ≈65-69% even with ground-truth segment pixels (54.8-57.6% with predicted segments) — a PCK@5px≥0.95 gate is not achieved by SOTA soccer-field calibration even in-domain with GT input; pickleball's simpler rectangle-only geometry may behave differently but this is unproven.

'No Bells, Just Whistles' (CVPR-W 2024, arXiv 2404.08401v2) shows plain classical DLT+RANSAC calibration (NO learned refinement network) beats prior refinement-network SOTA on SoccerNet/WC14/TS-WorldCup, when fed a richer geometry-derived keypoint set (extra keypoints synthesized via line-intersection/conic constructions from the base annotated set) — but code is GPL-2.0 (copyleft, commercial-incompatible) and the upstream keypoint detectors still need 22,816+ labeled images to train.

PnLCalib (arXiv 2404.08401, CVIU 2026, paper CC BY 4.0 but code GPL-2.0) adds a nonlinear least-squares refinement using BOTH detected lines and points (cost = 0.6*line_term + 0.4*point_term) on top of any existing keypoint/line detector output, improving JaC@5px by ~5-8 points across all tested soccer datasets with no detector retraining required — directly answers 'how to fix a no-op refinement step' with a concrete, license-clean-to-reimplement objective.

MAGSAC++ (CVPR 2020) is a training-free, drop-in robust estimator (cv2.USAC_MAGSAC) that beats vanilla RANSAC on extreme-view-change homography fitting and is far less sensitive to its inlier threshold; Graph-Cut RANSAC reports ~10-15% accuracy gain, LO-RANSAC ~20% gain over vanilla RANSAC — all usable immediately with zero new labels.

Plumb-line/Hough-entropy self-calibration (arXiv 0810.4426) recovers lens radial distortion purely from straight lines already in the scene (i.e., our own court lines) by minimizing Hough-space entropy after correction — a classical, zero-label method directly applicable to our 'wide-lens distortion is first-class' constraint.

yastrebksv/TennisCourtDetector (MIT license) is trained on 8,841 images across hard/clay/grass courts using a <7px correctness threshold — the closest racket-sport precedent for sizing our own label budget and PCK gate, but it required thousands of labeled images across surface/venue variety, not ~100.

A 2024 paper on amateur/messy tennis-court footage (arXiv 2404.06977) documents that classical Hough+homography court detection 'struggled to generalize efficiently' on dirty/amateur courts and fixed this by layering ML shadow-removal and object-detection on top of (not replacing) the classical geometric pipeline — a close structural analog to our own classical-Hough + learned-refinement setup and its generalization failure.

None of the production line-calling vendors (Hawk-Eye, FOXTENN, IMG Arena, Bolt6) disclose their court-tracking/calibration algorithms in any of the sourced regulatory documents — these are certification/history records, not engineering specs, and give a target accuracy bar (mm-level, fixed multi-camera rigs) but no implementable method for a single moving phone camera.

facts: 8-10 fixed cameras around the court, 50/60Hz, each on a dedicated PC, wired isolated network, one control PC fuses multi-view images vs a 'virtual model of the court' to reconstruct 3D ball/player trajectories.
ITF-approved 14 Oct 2005 (offline/review use); Hawk-Eye Live (real-time) not certified until June 2020 — 14-year gap.

2004 lab ground-truth used a separate Phantom high-speed camera (2000Hz, 512x512px, 0.5x0.5m FOV, ~1mm/px) with ±0.9mm reference uncertainty — this is the metrology rig, not the production camera array, and is not disclosed to be the production accuracy figure in these two ITF docs.

No algorithm, calibration method, triangulation math, accuracy number, or license is disclosed in either ITF source read (line-calling.pdf or the PAT-13-002 approval report) — both are regulatory/procedural, not engineering documents.

Patent US11893808B2 (filed Nov 2020, granted Feb 2024): single 2D phone camera; neural net correlates object-of-interest pixels with 'regulation topology' (known court-line geometry) pixels to infer 3D properties end-to-end, not via an explicit homography/keypoint-solver pipeline.

Training ground truth comes from co-located radar/Lidar sensors at multiple physically instrumented 'training events' over 'many hours of play' — a capital-intensive sensor-fusion data-acquisition regime we cannot replicate.
No accuracy numbers, network architecture, dataset size, or license/code are disclosed anywhere in the patent; it is a legal filing, not an engineering spec.

Sued by Infinity Cube Ltd. (maker of ITF-approved 'eyes3' hardware line-call system) for patent infringement, April 2022, S.D. California — establishes SwingVision as a software/phone-camera competitor operating in contested patent space, not itself ITF-certified for line calls.

Requires a single STATIC camera (tripod/fence/stable mount, ≥5ft height), all 4 court corners visible for the entire clip, landscape only, 1080p30 minimum (4K/60 gated behind paid tier), H.264/.mp4, HDR and image stabilization explicitly disabled.

Explicitly rejects multi-angle/tournament-cut footage: 'our AI requires that the same shot be seen throughout the entire video' — confirms no per-cut/per-segment re-calibration exists in their pipeline; one fixed extrinsic calibration is assumed for the whole video.

Ships 'CourtFocus,' an in-app real-time lock-on UI that guides the user to center the court BEFORE recording begins — alignment is gated at capture time, not solved purely by post-hoc arbitrary-viewpoint calibration.
No architecture, training-data regime, or license/accuracy numbers are disclosed on this help-center page; server-side processing takes ~30 minutes per clip (not real-time).

systems_intel.system: FOXTENN Top Real Precision System / FOXTENN Live
facts: Ground-level synchronized high-speed cameras + laser scanners at line ends imaging up to 2500 frames/second, directly capturing the ball's actual contact mark on the court surface at bounce — a non-inferential, non-trajectory-estimation approach fundamentally different from Hawk-Eye.
ITF-approved Dec 2016 (review mode); FOXTENN Live approved Nov 2021; first tournament use 2017 ATP Moselle Open.

Fuses two sensing modalities: 4 high-resolution cameras plus a patented 30-microphone acoustic-triangulation array analyzing ball-impact sound signatures for bounce localization, rather than relying on vision alone.

Permanent multi-fixed-camera installation (full-court + net + surrounding coverage), ~$20,000/court install cost plus monthly fee, oriented mainly at stroke/tactic/player-statistics analytics rather than solely automated line-calling — a fundamentally different permanently-installed-multicam product model vs a single moving phone.

systems_intel.system: ITF phased certification protocol (structural pattern, not a vision method)
facts: Nov 2003 ITF/WTA/ATP protocol defines 3 phases: (1) lab accuracy/usability test under controlled conditions, (2) 'shadow' test at a live tournament (calls recorded, not used by officials), (3) 'live' test where officials actually act on the system's calls.

Multiple independent vendor architectures (Hawk-Eye, FOXTENN, IMG Arena/FlightScope, Bolt6) all passed the same protocol, implying the certification bar is architecture-agnostic even though the document discloses no per-vendor method — only the phased validation structure itself, not any vision technique, is transferable to us.

method_shortlist.method: TVCalib-style differentiable segment-reprojection-loss camera calibration (replace homography 'refine' with direct pose optimization from line/point segments)
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Fixes the booked no-op/harmful refinement defect: paper's own ablation shows decompose-then-refine (structurally like ours) is measurably worse (AC@5 48.1%) than direct reprojection-loss optimization (68.7% GT-segments); solves full camera pose (FoV+pan/tilt/roll+t) from our existing Hough line + corner-tap evidence with a small (~5 scalar) per-frame optimizer.
method_shortlist.data_needed: None — the optimizer itself requires zero training data, only line/point pixel correspondences we already produce classically.
method_shortlist.effort: Medium (reimplement segment-reprojection loss + AdamW optimizer loop; pickleball geometry is simpler than soccer, no circle/point-cloud segments needed).
method_shortlist.license: MIT (github.com/mm4spa/tvcalib code); pretrained segmentation weights license unstated — don't reuse those, only the optimizer design.

method_shortlist.method: Multi-initialization + argmin robust optimization (run pose optimizer from several priors per frame, keep lowest-loss result)
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Concrete, cheap fix for local-minima/bad-init sensitivity of any gradient-based per-frame court solver on a moving camera where pose varies frame to frame; TVCalib shows this beats single fixed-init.
method_shortlist.effort: Low (re-run existing solver 3-5x with different pose priors, keep best).

method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Ready template for the product's 'honest uncertainty' requirement: reject/flag a frame's court-lock (and downstream in/out call) when final reprojection loss exceeds an empirically tuned threshold, trading completeness for accuracy — exactly the abstention behavior the product needs.
method_shortlist.data_needed: A small validation set (we already have some) to tune tau; no new labeled venues required.
method_shortlist.license: MIT (TVCalib pattern; concept itself is not proprietary).

method_shortlist.method: PnLCalib-style points+lines nonlinear least-squares refinement (cost = alpha*line_dist + (1-alpha)*point_reproj, alpha~0.6)
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: A concrete, well-specified refinement objective to replace the broken homography-refine step, using BOTH our classical Hough line evidence and corner-tap/solver points; improved JaC@5px by ~5-8pts across all tested soccer datasets with no detector retraining.
method_shortlist.data_needed: None — refinement runs on top of existing point/line detections, no training.
method_shortlist.effort: Low-Medium (reimplement the weighted nonlinear least-squares cost; do not import GPL code).
method_shortlist.license: Code is GPL-2.0 (copyleft, commercial-incompatible) — reimplement the objective from the paper (CC BY 4.0) rather than vendoring the repo.

method_shortlist.method: MAGSAC++ / Graph-Cut RANSAC / LO-RANSAC as drop-in robust estimator for homography/pose fitting
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Training-free upgrade over vanilla RANSAC for fitting court geometry from noisy line/point correspondences; MAGSAC++ least sensitive to inlier-threshold choice, GC-RANSAC ~10-15% and LO-RANSAC ~20% accuracy gains reported over vanilla RANSAC on homography tasks.
method_shortlist.effort: Low (MAGSAC++ is a one-line OpenCV flag change: cv2.USAC_MAGSAC).
method_shortlist.license: OpenCV (BSD) for cv2.USAC_MAGSAC; GC-RANSAC/LO-RANSAC academic implementations need per-repo license check before commercial use.

method_shortlist.method: Hierarchical/virtual geometric keypoint expansion from known court dimensions (derive extra keypoints via line-intersection and construction from the base annotated set, no new labels)
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: 'No Bells, Just Whistles' shows adding geometrically-derived keypoint subsets (not new labels, just constructions from existing points) monotonically improves completeness/final-score even when raw per-point accuracy dips slightly — directly portable to pickleball's rectangle+kitchen-line geometry using only our existing manual corner taps.
method_shortlist.data_needed: None — synthesized from existing court model/taps.
method_shortlist.license: Algorithmic idea only (repo itself is GPL-2.0, do not vendor code) — reimplement from the paper's described construction.

method_shortlist.method: Plumb-line / minimal-Hough-entropy self-calibration of lens radial distortion from court lines already in frame
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Classical, zero-label recovery of phone wide-lens radial distortion using the straight court lines we already detect, directly matching the 'distortion is first-class' constraint without a calibration target or labeled dataset.
method_shortlist.effort: Medium (implement Hough-entropy minimization search over distortion parameter).
method_shortlist.license: Academic paper, no code license stated — reimplement from method description.

method_shortlist.method: ELSED / DeepLSD subpixel line-segment detection as classical line evidence source
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Faster/more precise line-segment extraction than basic Hough for feeding the reprojection-loss or points+lines refinement stages; ELSED is fast and permissively licensed, DeepLSD combines learned robustness with handcrafted geometric accuracy via pretrained weights (no pickleball-specific retraining implied by the corpus, since it's not scene-specific).
method_shortlist.data_needed: None for off-the-shelf use (pretrained weights ship with DeepLSD); no pickleball-specific labels needed to try it as a drop-in detector.
method_shortlist.effort: Low (both are usable off-the-shelf; DeepLSD needs a GPU-inference integration).
method_shortlist.license: ELSED: Apache-2.0 (commercial-safe). DeepLSD: MIT (repo), but pretrained-weight license not independently confirmed in this corpus.

method_shortlist.method: CourtFocus-style capture-time alignment gating (pre-recording lock-on UX, product pattern not a model)
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Directly copies pb.vision's production strategy of pushing viewpoint-robustness burden onto capture UX (guide user to a good, stable alignment moment) rather than only solving arbitrary in-motion tracking post-hoc — reduces the practical severity of the temporal-tracking gap for the L0 on-device advisory experience.
method_shortlist.effort: Low-Medium (UI/UX work, not a CV model change).
method_shortlist.license: N/A (own implementation of a UX pattern, not a copied artifact).

method_shortlist.method: Phased internal validation gate modeled on ITF lab->shadow->live protocol
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Adopt an analogous internal gate: bench/synthetic accuracy test -> shadow test against owner's own live sessions (no product-facing calls) -> live in/out calls — a process/rigor pattern, not a vision technique, for de-risking promotion of any court-tracking improvement.
method_shortlist.data_needed: None beyond what we already capture; no new labeled venues required to define the gate structure itself.

method_shortlist.method: yastrebksv/TennisCourtDetector-style 14-keypoint CNN fine-tuned on our ~100 new diverse-venue frames (transfer learning, not from-scratch)
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Closest racket-sport precedent for a small-label-budget learned keypoint detector (their <7px threshold, MIT license) — a realistic template for how far ~100 owner labels could push a small CNN if used as fine-tuning data on top of an ImageNet/court-family-pretrained backbone, rather than training a new 21M-parameter network from scratch (which we already measured memorizes venue families).
method_shortlist.data_needed: ~100 owner-labeled diverse-venue frames (matches the staged batch) as a fine-tuning/held-out-eval set, not a from-scratch training set.
method_shortlist.effort: Medium-High (fine-tuning + held-out cross-venue eval to check whether 100 labels actually move the 290px cross-source number).

method_shortlist.method: KpSFR-style dense uniform-grid keypoints (dynamic-filter instance segmentation) for viewpoint-robustness under partial occlusion
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Addresses cases where classic sparse corners are out-of-frame/occluded but denser grid points are still visible — directly relevant to our viewpoint-diversity PCK@5px gate design, since it targets exactly the failure mode (few classic corners visible) our cross-source-transfer wall exhibits.
method_shortlist.data_needed: Requires a labeled dataset at TS-WorldCup scale (3,812 images) to train from scratch in the original paper — our ~100-label batch is far below this; would need heavy transfer-learning/augmentation to be viable, and is unproven for pickleball's simpler line geometry.
method_shortlist.license: Not confirmed in corpus (needs direct repo check before any commercial use).

method_shortlist.method: Quadrilateral Constraint Module / hard known-geometry constraint during/after keypoint refinement (CourtKeyNet pattern)
method_shortlist.what_it_buys: Enforces pickleball's known, fixed 44x20ft (+kitchen) rectangular geometry as a hard constraint on any keypoint output, which should be directly implementable as a post-processing/optimization constraint (project detected points back onto a valid parameterized rectangle) using our own existing court model, independent of the learned-network specifics in the source paper.
method_shortlist.data_needed: None for the constraint-enforcement idea itself (it operates on whatever keypoints we already produce, classical or learned); the full CourtKeyNet learned backbone would need its own labeled badminton-scale dataset, which we are not adopting.
method_shortlist.effort: Low-Medium for the constraint-only version; High if adopting the full learned architecture.
method_shortlist.license: Not stated in corpus for CourtKeyNet code (paper only, ScienceDirect); treat as unconfirmed for any code reuse.

claim_verdicts_summary: [CORROBORATED] Hawk-Eye Officiating (ITF-certified) is a fixed multi-camera (8-10, 50/60Hz) rig with per-camera PCs on an isolated network and one control PC fusing multi-view images against a virtual court model — sourced directly from ITF's own PAT-13-002 approval report.

[CORROBORATED] Hawk-Eye's Dec 2004 lab test used a separate Phantom high-speed camera (2000Hz, 512x512px, 0.5x0.5m FOV, ~1mm/px) with ±0.9mm reference uncertainty as ground truth, using compressed-air-cannon and BOLA-projector ball delivery — sourced directly from the ITF line-calling.pdf primary document, with exact numbers quoted.
[CORROBORATED] Hawk-Eye Officiating approved 14 Oct 2005 / first used Hopman Cup Jan 2006; Hawk-Eye Live approved June 2020 — dates directly stated in the ITF document.

[CORROBORATED] SwingVision's patent (US11893808B2) describes a single-phone-camera neural network correlating object pixels with court-line 'regulation topology' pixels, trained against radar/Lidar ground truth at instrumented events — directly quoted/paraphrased from the patent text itself, filing/publication dates confirmed (filed 2020-11-30, published 2024-02-06).

[CORROBORATED] pb.vision requires a single static camera, ≥5ft mount, all-4-corners visibility, no camera cuts, and ships a CourtFocus pre-recording lock-on UI — directly sourced from pb.vision's own help-center article with exact figures (5ft, 1080p30, H.264, 4Mbps, 5GB, 30min).

[CORROBORATED] TVCalib's calibration/optimization module requires zero training data and is a per-image differentiable segment-reprojection-loss optimizer (AdamW, 2000 steps) over camera pose parameters, with code released under MIT license — confirmed via both the arXiv paper and the GitHub repo license file.

[CORROBORATED] TVCalib's own ablation shows homography-decompose-then-refine baselines underperform direct reprojection-loss camera-parameter optimization (e.g., AC@5 48.1% HDecomp vs 68.7% TVCalib on GT segments, SN-Calib-test-center) — numeric comparison quoted directly from the paper's Table 2/3.

[CORROBORATED] 'No Bells, Just Whistles' achieves SOTA calibration accuracy using classical DLT+RANSAC with NO learned refinement network, but its code repository is licensed GPL-2.0 (copyleft) — confirmed by directly fetching the repo's LICENSE file, distinct from the paper's own CC BY 4.0 license which covers only the text.

[CORROBORATED] PnLCalib's points+lines refinement (cost = 0.6*line + 0.4*point terms) improves JaC@5px by roughly 5-8 percentage points across SoccerNet/WC14/TS-WorldCup versus a points-only baseline, requiring no detector retraining — numbers quoted directly from the paper's tables; code is GPL-2.0, paper is CC BY 4.0.

[CORROBORATED] MAGSAC++ is a training-free, faster, and inlier-threshold-insensitive robust estimator that outperforms vanilla RANSAC on extreme-view-change homography fitting, drop-in via cv2.USAC_MAGSAC — sourced from the CVPR 2020 paper's stated results.

[UNVERIFIED] The claim that SoccerNet/WC14 field-registration numbers (e.g., AC@5px ~65-69% ceiling) would transfer proportionally to pickleball's simpler rectangle-only court geometry is an inference by the research corpus, not measured on any racket-sport or pickleball dataset in any source read — flagged explicitly as unproven in the corpus's own applicability notes.

[UNVERIFIED] Whether pb.vision's actual ~5.67px court reprojection (our own external observation, not from their help page) is attributable primarily to their capture-discipline UX (static camera, CourtFocus gating) versus an undisclosed superior algorithm is inferred/speculative — pb.vision discloses no architecture, training regime, or accuracy numbers on the fetched help-center page.

[UNVERIFIED] The claim that Hawk-Eye's often-cited '2.6mm' production accuracy figure is the actual live in-match error (versus being solely the historical 2004 lab-rig ground-truth-uncertainty figure of ±0.9mm) is not disambiguated by either ITF document read — the sourced documents give the lab-test uncertainty and a phased certification history, but do not state a single definitive current production accuracy number, so any specific 'Hawk-Eye = X mm' figure elsewhere in circulation should be treated as unconfirmed by these primary sources.

[CORROBORATED] yastrebksv/TennisCourtDetector is MIT-licensed, trained on 8,841 images across hard/clay/grass surfaces with a <7px correctness threshold — confirmed via the repo's stated dataset/license details.
[UNVERIFIED] KpSFR's and CourtKeyNet's code licensing terms are not confirmed in this corpus (flagged explicitly by the scout as 'needs direct check before use') and must not be assumed permissive.
synthesis.now_vs_after_labels: Implementable with ZERO new labels (classical/geometric or training-free, usable immediately on the existing stack): replacing the broken homography-refinement step with a TVCalib-style differentiable segment-reprojection-loss pose optimizer (MIT, no training data needed) or a PnLCalib-style points+lines nonlinear-least-squares refinement (reimplement the objective — code is GPL-2.0 so don't vendor it, but the CC-BY-4.0 paper's formula is free to reimplement); swapping vanilla RANSAC for MAGSAC++ (training-free, one-line OpenCV change) or reimplementing GC-RANSAC/LO-RANSAC patterns; adding multi-initialization+argmin robustness and a reprojection-loss-threshold self-verification/abstention gate (both zero-label patterns from TVCalib, and the latter directly serves the product's honest-uncertainty requirement); synthesizing extra "virtual" geometric keypoints from our existing corner taps via line-intersection constructions (the "No Bells" trick, reimplemented to avoid its GPL license); plumb-line/Hough-entropy self-calibration of lens radial distortion straight from our own court lines (no calibration target needed); adopting ELSED/DeepLSD as a faster or more precise off-the-shelf line-segment detector feeding these stages; copying pb.vision's CourtFocus capture-time alignment-gating UX pattern; and structuring an internal ITF-style lab->shadow->live phased validation gate for any of the above. What needs the ~100 owner labels (or more): fine-tuning a small racket-sport-precedented keypoint CNN (yastrebksv-style, MIT-licensed, <7px threshold) as a true held-out cross-venue eval to test whether 100 labels move our measured 290px cross-source-transfer number at all — this is the most direct test of the labeling bet, but the corpus gives no racket-sport evidence that 100 labels (versus thousands, as every SOTA soccer/tennis keypoint detector used) is sufficient; and any move toward denser grid-keypoint architectures (KpSFR) or novel constraint-network architectures (CourtKeyNet) that were only validated in the source corpus on thousand-image-scale datasets, whose code licenses are additionally unconfirmed. What needs gold/instrumented capture beyond even 100 labels: SwingVision's own disclosed method (radar/Lidar-instrumented training events) and Hawk-Eye/FOXTENN/Zenniz's fixed multi-camera or multi-sensor (audio+vision) rigs are both structurally inapplicable to a single moving phone and would require capital/hardware investments (dedicated sensor rigs, fixed installations) outside the labeling-budget question entirely — none of these production competitors offer a monocular-moving-camera method we can adopt at any label count, which is itself the clearest finding: no primary source in this corpus discloses a working single-moving-camera production court-tracking algorithm; every disclosed production system is either fixed-multi-camera (Hawk-Eye, FOXTENN, PlaySight, Zenniz) or a black-box patent with no method detail (SwingVision), or explicitly a single-STATIC-camera product (pb.vision) — so closing our per-frame temporal tracking gap for a moving camera has no direct production precedent to copy at all, labels or not.
open_questions: Would pickleball's simpler rectangle-only court geometry (no circles/arcs, fewer independent line constraints than a soccer pitch) make TVCalib/PnLCalib/No-Bells-style reprojection-loss calibration hit a materially higher or lower AC@5px ceiling than the ~65-69% observed on soccer — no source in this corpus tests any racket-sport or pickleball-scale court with these methods.

Is 100 owner-labeled diverse-venue frames enough to meaningfully move our measured 290px cross-source keypoint-transfer failure, given every SOTA racket-sport/soccer keypoint detector in this corpus (yastrebksv: 8,841 images; SoccerNet-Calibration: 22,816 images; TS-WorldCup: 3,812 images) was trained/fine-tuned on far larger label counts than we plan to collect?

What is Hawk-Eye's actual disclosed production (not lab-rig) accuracy figure and underlying algorithm — neither ITF document read discloses camera-count-specific triangulation, calibration, or Kalman-filtering method, or a single authoritative current production accuracy number, so any 'Hawk-Eye = 2.6mm' claim circulating elsewhere remains unconfirmed by primary sources fetched here.

Does DeepLSD's or SOLD2's pretrained line-detector actually generalize to pickleball court lines/wide-lens-distorted phone footage without any pickleball-specific fine-tuning, or would it need at least some of the ~100 labeled frames to validate — the corpus asserts pretrained-weight availability but does not test on racket-sport or distorted-lens imagery.
Is CourtKeyNet's or KpSFR's code available under a commercially usable license — flagged explicitly as unconfirmed in the corpus and must be checked directly before any adoption decision.

Would combining the points+lines refinement objective (PnLCalib) with our existing manual-corner-tap + Hough-line evidence actually fix the measured homography-refinement-is-a-no-op-or-worse defect, or is the defect rooted in something in our own pipeline (e.g., a bug) that no external method would fix regardless — this requires direct debugging of our own refinement code, not just adopting a new external objective.

claimVerdicts.claim: Hawk-Eye's production deployment (ITF-certified) uses multiple fixed cameras -- typically 8-10 -- at 50/60Hz mounted around the court, each wired via Ethernet to a dedicated PC on an isolated network, with a control PC running proprietary software that reconstructs ball/player 3D trajectories against 'a virtual model of the court.'

read_facts: ITF/WTA/ATP November 2003 protocol defines a 3-phase evaluation: (1) lab accuracy + usability demo under controlled conditions, (2) 'shadow' test at a live tournament where calls are recorded but not used by officials, (3) 'live' test where officials actually use the system's calls.

Hawk-Eye's Dec 2004 lab test used a ground-truth reference rig, NOT the production Hawk-Eye camera array itself: a single Phantom high-speed camera placed at 4 separate ball-impact locations around the court, used only to establish independently-measured ball-impact ground truth against which Hawk-Eye's own multi-camera triangulation output was compared.

Ground-truth camera was mounted ~1 cm above the court surface (near edge-on to the surface plane) to capture the ball-to-line impact directly, at 2,000 Hz with a 50-microsecond shutter to freeze motion.

Ground-truth imaging used a calibrated field of view of 0.5 x 0.5 m mapped to a 512x512 pixel sensor, i.e. ~1mm/pixel physical resolution at the impact zone -- test rig accuracy is bounded by pixel-scale calibration of a small, tightly-controlled FOV, not by wide-scene reprojection.

Ball delivery for the accuracy test used two independent projectile methods to vary speed/angle/spin at the line: a compressed-air cannon at ~30 degrees to horizontal firing zero-spin balls at 30-40 m/s (2 of 4 sites), and a dual-wheel BOLA ball machine firing from ~2m height across the net at 40-50 m/s (other 2 of 4 sites) -- controlled, repeatable, varied physical inputs rather than real match footage, used purely to stress-test line-proximity accuracy.

The document does not disclose Hawk-Eye's internal algorithm (camera count, triangulation method, court calibration procedure, ball-tracking/Kalman filtering) -- it is a certification/history document, not a technical/engineering spec of the vision pipeline itself.

No licensing, open-source, code, or model-weights information is given for any system (Hawk-Eye, FOXTENN, IMG Arena/FlightScope, Bolt6) -- all are described purely as commercial/proprietary certified products with approval dates, no method disclosure.

Multiple independent vendors (Hawk-Eye, FOXTENN, IMG Arena/FlightScope, Bolt6 Sentinel) have each passed the same standardized ITF lab+shadow+live protocol, meaning several different hardware/vision architectures can all hit line-calling-grade accuracy -- suggesting the certification is architecture-agnostic and pipeline design is left to each vendor.
Camera count: typically 8-10 cameras mounted at fixed locations around the court.
Frame rate: typically 50/60 Hz (high frame rate).
Each camera connects via Ethernet cable to a dedicated PC that performs image processing on (and optionally stores) the raw image data -- i.e., per-camera edge processing before central fusion.
All camera PCs plus one additional 'control' PC are connected on a wired, isolated (non-internet-facing) network for security/anti-hacking reasons.

The control PC starts/stops data capture, aggregates per-camera-PC outputs, and runs proprietary software to reconstruct 3D ball and player trajectories using camera images fused with 'a virtual model of the court' (i.e., a pre-built court geometry model used as a fusion prior/reference frame).
A second proprietary software package (typically on a separate PC, run by a separate operator) turns the 3D trajectory data into virtual replay graphics and derived match statistics.

Output data is delivered to in-stadium display and TV broadcast via one-directional coaxial video cable (preventing any downstream access back into the system); internet distribution goes through a dedicated PC on the isolated network feeding a web app ('Pulse') via FTP to a centralized password-protected server.
System operates autonomously with no player input required; typically staffed by 2-3 human operators.

Core method: a neural network (element 160) is trained to correlate two co-occurring pixel-arrangement types in a single 2D camera frame - pixels of the 'object of interest' (ball/racket/player) and pixels of 'regulation topology' (known, standardized court lines: baseline, sidelines, service lines) - and from that correlation infer 3D properties of the object (3D location in event space, orientation, size, velocity, and derived scoring events like faults/fouls/balls-strikes).

Training-time ground truth is NOT derived from the phone camera: multiple 3D sensors (radar and/or Lidar) are co-located with a single training camera at 'training events,' and 3D measurements from those sensors are timestamped and paired with the simultaneous 2D camera images to form (2D image, 3D measurement) supervision pairs.

Training data is gathered 'over many hours of play' across 'multiple similarly outfitted training events' (i.e., multiple physical venues/events each instrumented with radar/Lidar + camera), implying the model is meant to generalize across the several instrumented venues used during data collection, not a single fixed court.

Because the reference feature is the court's 'regulation topology' (the fixed, rule-defined dimensions of a real court), the network can use the known real-world geometry of the lines visible in-frame as an implicit scale/pose reference, analogous to using the court's line geometry as a calibration target rather than needing per-venue extrinsic calibration hardware.

Deployment-time inference uses only the single on-device 2D camera; the app 'adjusts its geometry accordingly' to the camera's mounting position/angle, implying some per-session geometric adjustment exists at inference, but the patent gives no algorithmic detail (no homography/PnP/keypoint-solver specifics disclosed).

The patent explicitly frames the neural network as trained end-to-end to map (2D image containing object + court lines) -> 3D property, rather than as a separate two-stage classical-calibration-then-lift pipeline; it does not disclose a distinct court-corner-detection or homography-refinement module.

No specific neural network architecture is disclosed beyond generic language: 'model trainer 430 initializes and iteratively updates a set of model parameters 432... including node structure parameters and weights of connections among the nodes' - i.e., no CNN/transformer specifics, no backbone name, no input resolution.
No quantitative accuracy/error metrics, dataset sizes, frame rates, or image resolutions are disclosed anywhere in the patent text.
Camera must be mounted "at least 5 feet off the ground using a tripod, fence mount, or stable platform" — no handheld capture allowed.
"The entire court, including all 4 corners, must be clearly visible in the frame" — full-court, all-corners framing is a hard requirement, not advisory.
Wide-angle view from center or corner preferred; any angle acceptable as long as the full court and players remain visible throughout the clip.

CourtFocus: an in-app (Android/iPhone) lock-on UI feature that guides the user to "perfectly center your court using a built-in lock-on indicator" before recording — alignment is enforced/gated at capture time via UI feedback, not corrected purely after the fact in post-processing.
Camera must be static for the entire clip: "The camera must be stable and not move during filming" / "Avoid handheld footage" — single fixed viewpoint for the whole video is a hard requirement.

Tournament/broadcast footage with camera cuts is explicitly unsupported: "most videos from tournaments cut to multiple different camera angles. Our AI requires that the same shot be seen throughout the entire video" — confirms their pipeline has no per-cut re-calibration / multi-shot handling; it assumes one fixed extrinsic calibration for the whole video.
Recording orientation: landscape mode only.

Users are told to disable phone-side image stabilization and HDR when not using the PB Vision capture app ("disabling image stabilization or any other special settings such as HDR") — implies their court/pose pipeline is sensitive to stabilization-induced crop/warp changes and HDR tone-mapping artifacts, and prefers raw, geometrically-consistent frames.

read_facts: Pipeline stages (Fig.1): (1) Semantic/instance Segmentation (DeepLabv3-ResNet-101) labels each visible field line/circle/goal-post segment in the raw image; (2) Point Selection is a non-differentiable post-processing step choosing >=2 representative pixels per line segment (found empirically that >2 points gives more stable gradients; hyperparams |Nline|=4, |Npc|=8 sampled points); (3) Calibration Module: gradient-based iterative optimization (AdamW, lr=0.05, weight_decay=0.01, one-cycle LR schedule pct_start=0.5, 2000 optimization steps) directly minimizing the 'segment reprojection loss' over camera parameters phi=(FoV,t,pan,tilt,roll) -- NO neural network predicts the camera pose, it is solved per-image by gradient descent from an initialization; (4) Self-Verification rejects any sample whose final loss exceeds a threshold tau (tau=0.019 chosen globally by maximizing Compound Score on SN-Calib-valid-center).

Camera model: standard pinhole P = K R [I|-t]; square pixels, zero skew, principal point fixed at image center; predicts Field-of-View (FoV) instead of focal length directly and normalizes coordinates to NDC [-1,1] for numerical stability; rotation parameterized as R = Rz(roll) Rx(tilt) Rz(pan) (3 Euler angles) plus translation t -- so only 5 scalars (FoV,pan,tilt,roll,+3D t) are optimized per image/frame, no deep network weights.

Calibration object is explicitly 'keypoint-less': it is the union of labeled SEGMENTS (line segments s_line={X0+lambda*X1}, point-cloud segments s_pc e.g. circles sampled to 128 3D points with ~0.45m point density, and plain point segments s_point for classic PnP) rather than sparse keypoints -- the paper's stated motivation is that individual field keypoints are frequently NOT VISIBLE in broadcast frames but the lines/segments they lie on usually are.

Loss (Eq.1-2): segment reprojection loss = mean over all segment categories S of the mean distance d between each annotated/predicted pixel and its reprojected 3D segment; for a line segment this is the point-to-line PERPENDICULAR distance (a determinant-based formula, Eq.1); for a point-cloud segment it is the MIN Euclidean distance from the pixel to any reprojected cloud point; distances for non-visible/padded segments are masked to zero; every segment contributes EQUALLY to the loss (not weighted by pixel count) so bigger/richer segments don't dominate.

Handles optional radial lens distortion jointly: distort_psi(x)/undistort(x) functions (kornia lens-distortion model, psi={k1,k2}) can be optimized with a SEPARATE AdamW optimizer (lr=1e-3, one-cycle pct_start=0.33) run after the main camera-parameter optimization -- treated as an add-on, not baked into the main loss from the start, because joint optimization from scratch causes local minima (esp. low-FoV images) per their ablation.

Multiple/random restarts: because camera parameters (esp. translation) have large variance across broadcast setups, TVCalib runs the SAME optimization from MULTIPLE random initializations sampled from a coarse prior distribution per camera type (center/left/right tribune position), then picks the result with minimum final loss (argmin) as the output -- this is their solution to local minima from a single bad init, and 'argmin' multi-init is shown to beat single-init 'center' and even matches/slightly beats knowing the true camera-position class ('stacked').

Homography is recovered as a special case (when the 3D calibration-object plane Z=0): H = P[:, [1,2,4]] = K R[:, [1,2]] [I|-t] -- i.e. TVCalib always solves full 3D camera calibration and homography drops out for free, rather than the conventional pipeline of estimating a 2D homography first and only afterward decomposing it (which the paper shows is lossy: 'Homography Decomposition' baseline (HDecomp) is measurably worse than direct calibration).

Contrasts explicitly with the dominant paradigm of 'initial homography estimate -> iterative refinement' (e.g., Lucas-Kanade alignment, spatial-transformer-network refinement) used by prior sports-registration systems; TVCalib instead does single-step direct optimization of true camera parameters with no separate refinement stage and NO training data required for the calibration step itself (only the upstream segmentation network needs training data).

Pipeline = 3 stages: (1) segment localization via DeepLabV3+ResNet instance segmentation predicting which pixels belong to each labeled field primitive (lines, circles/point-clouds, goal posts/points); (2) point selection — post-processing picks >=2 max-separated pixels per line segment (more points kept when a curved polyline is needed for lens-distortion fitting); (3) calibration module — differentiable segment-reprojection-loss minimized by gradient descent to directly recover camera parameters phi in one step (no separate homography-then-refine stage, unlike prior art).

Calibration object is modeled with known-3D-dimension primitives — point, line (parametric X0+λX1), and point-cloud segments — rather than sparse keypoints; explicitly designed because keypoints are frequently non-visible/occluded in broadcast frames whereas line/area markings almost always are.

Camera model: pinhole P=KR[I|-t]; assumes square pixels, zero skew, principal point at image center; the only intrinsic unknown solved for is Field-of-View (not raw focal length), reprojected via Normalized Device Coordinates for numerical stability; extrinsics = pan/tilt/roll Euler angles + translation t. Full parameter vector phi={FoV, t, pan, tilt, roll}. Optional differentiable radial lens-distortion coefficients psi can be jointly optimized with phi (not assumed a priori, model-agnostic distort/undistort functions).

Loss (Eq.2): mean point-to-line / point-to-point-cloud Euclidean reprojection distance between annotated/predicted pixels and the reprojected 3D segment, averaged so every visible segment contributes equally regardless of pixel count; minimized over both phi and psi jointly via AdamW (lr=0.05, weight decay 0.01, 2000 optimization steps, one-cycle LR schedule with pct_start=0.5).

Initialization handles ambiguity via priors: raw camera-parameter ranges are assumed uniform U(a,b), converted to a normal distribution N(mu,sigma) covering the 95% CI, then standardized to start near zero (roughly = a mean/central view of the calibration object) rather than hand-picking per-frame starting values.

Multiple-initialization strategy: because camera position (e.g., center/left/right stands) may have large variance, the method runs the optimizer from several candidate starting camera positions and either (a) keeps the lowest-loss result ("argmin"), or (b) uses the known camera-type-specific start ("stacked") if that prior is available — both variants beat a single fixed center-only initialization.

Self-verification/abstention: images are rejected outright (no calibration returned) if the final segment-reprojection loss exceeds an empirically chosen threshold tau (tuned by maximizing a Compound Score on a validation split; tau≈0.019, explored range 0.013–0.025); this trades completeness ratio (CR, fraction of images calibrated) against accuracy on the accepted subset — explicit accuracy-vs-abstention knob.

No temporal/video consistency is modeled — each frame is calibrated independently; the paper explicitly lists 'integration of temporal consistency and associated speedup' as unimplemented future work.

Pipeline: (1) geometry-based keypoint generation derived from SoccerNet annotations -- defines a fixed grid of keypoints/lines from known court geometry (line intersections, ellipse/circle fitting for center circle and penalty arcs); keypoints organized into hierarchical subsets Kp (base/main, matching Cioppa et al. segment definitions), Kpe (line extremities), Kp1, Kp2, Kp3 -- each subsequent subset derived from intersections/constructions using the prior subset's points.

(2) Two separate encoder-decoder neural nets (modified HRNetV2-w48 backbone) predict heatmaps: one for keypoints (single Gaussian peak, 2px sigma) and one for line extremities; positions extracted via max-pooling on heatmaps; softmax/sigmoid final activations for keypoints/lines respectively.

(3) A 'keypoints mask' handles insufficient-point cases; keypoint disambiguation step enumerates candidate correspondence combinations (for symmetric/ambiguous field regions) and keeps the one minimizing reprojection error, using cross-ratio/vector consistency to pick the correct top-view-consistent combination.

(4) Final step is 'classical camera calibration through DLT (Direct Linear Transform) algorithm in a minimalist fashion, without further refinement,' combined with RANSAC for outlier rejection -- explicitly NO learned refinement network runs after DLT (unlike prior works [6,7,28] which use homography-refinement networks).

Full 3D calibration (not just 2D homography): projection matrix P = K R [I | -c] in R^{3x4}; two extra vertical planes modeled for goal polygons to place non-planar keypoints (goal posts/crossbars); ground-plane calibration is used as fallback and results transformed to ground-plane coordinates when insufficient ground-plane points are visible.

Ablation heuristics for keypoint subset choice at calibration time: 'full-keypoints' (all of Kp, Kpe, Kp1, Kp2, Kp3), 'main-keypoints' (Cioppa et al. segment-only annotations), and 'ground-plane-keypoints' (excludes non-planar points); a grid of RANSAC reprojection-error thresholds was swept per subset, with full-keypoints yielding lowest reprojection error.

Two model variants trained from scratch: Multi-view (MV, 200 epochs, lr=1e-2, batch=2) and Single-view (SV, 200 epochs, lr=1e-5, batch=2), Adam optimizer (beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999), L2 heatmap-regression loss, data augmentation = random horizontal flip, color jitter, Gaussian noise; trained on single RTX 2080 Ti (12GB). SV models further fine-tuned on WC14 and TSWC by converting their GT homographies into the paper's keypoint/line-extremity format.

Explicit stated condition for method to work: 'As long as the video frame distortions are not too harsh (i.e. fisheye shots) and a minimum of four keypoints are visible, sports field registration is shown to be effective' -- authors flag fisheye/strong-distortion frames as a known failure mode, and state future work would add temporal consistency across video frames (not yet implemented in this paper).

Pipeline: (1) HRNet-based keypoint heatmap detector predicts confidence heatmaps for a predefined set of 3D-field keypoint classes; (2) a separate line-detection component extracts field line segments from the image; (3) an initial camera pose/homography is estimated from the detected keypoints (PnP-style, as in prior NLCalib work); (4) a novel non-linear least-squares REFINEMENT module ('PnL') jointly optimizes camera parameters against BOTH detected line segments and detected keypoints, instead of points alone.

Refinement cost function: C = alpha * sum(d(l_i, l_detected_i)) + (1-alpha) * sum(d(x_bar_detected_j, x_j)) -- a weighted sum of line-distance and point-reprojection-distance terms, minimized by nonlinear least-squares; alpha is set empirically to 0.6 (lines weighted higher than points).

Camera model: standard full perspective projection P = K R [I | -t] in R^{3x4}; K has focal length {alpha_x, alpha_y} and principal point {x0,y0}; assumes zero skew and known/fixed pixel aspect ratio -- i.e. a simplified intrinsics model, not a full radial-distortion camera model in the base formulation.

Lens-distortion optimization exists only as a separate, later-added utility in the public codebase (utils/utils_calib_wp.py, added per repo history ~March 2026), i.e. distortion handling was bolted on after the original paper, not part of the core published optimization.

Single-view (SV) model is trained on SoccerNet-Calibration then fine-tuned per-target-dataset onto WorldCup2014 (WC14), TS-WorldCup (TSWC), and WorldPose; multi-view (MV) model uses one unified model across the whole SoccerNet distribution without venue-specific auxiliary models, contrasting with prior venue-dependent approaches.

Documented failure modes: close-up shots with few/no visible landmarks, and fisheye shots from inside the goal where extreme lens distortion makes calibration fail; performance also degrades on non-standard camera angles underrepresented in training data (visible in the full multi-view test set vs the main-camera-centered subset).

Domain is exclusively soccer/football broadcast video (single or multi fixed broadcast cameras at a known, standard-sized field) -- no other sport, and no discussion of a handheld/moving single camera use case.
Code released under GPL-2.0 license (copyleft); pretrained weights are distributed via GitHub releases and appear to follow the same GPL-2.0 terms as the code (no separate weights license stated).

read_facts: MAGSAC++ (Barath, Noskova, Ivashechkin, Matas; CVPR 2020, arXiv 1912.05909, submitted 11 Dec 2019) is a robust model estimator (RANSAC-family) that fits geometric models (homography, fundamental matrix, etc.) from noisy point/line correspondences WITHOUT any learned/trained component and without a user-set inlier-outlier threshold.

Core idea: treat the noise scale sigma as an unknown random variable, uniform on (0, sigma_max), and marginalize model quality and model parameters over sigma instead of picking one inlier/outlier threshold. Residuals of true inliers are modeled with a scaled chi-distribution with n degrees of freedom (n = dimension of the residual space, e.g. n=4 for point correspondences in the 4D joint image-pair space).

MAGSAC++ reformulates the sigma-marginalization as an Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) M-estimation problem: theta_{i+1} = argmin_theta sum_p w(D(theta_i,p)) * D(theta,p)^2, with per-point weight w(D) = integral over sigma of P(p|theta,sigma) f(sigma) dsigma. This avoids the many repeated least-squares refits over sampled sigma values that made the original MAGSAC (2019) slow.

A new quality (scoring) function Q(theta,P) = 1/L(theta,P) is defined, where L is an M-estimator loss with rho-function derived analytically in closed form (via gamma/incomplete-gamma functions) so it does not require the inlier-outlier decision at all.

Weight/quality computation is closed-form using complete and incomplete gamma functions; these can be precomputed and stored in a lookup table so weight+quality calculation is 'merely a few operations per point', making it fast.

tau(sigma) = k*sigma is the threshold implied by a chosen high quantile (e.g., 0.99) of the chi-distribution; for point correspondences (n=4 dims), k=3.64 (the 0.99 quantile). Running IRLS with this fixed threshold-noise relation is called 'sigma-consensus++.' sigma_max is a single user-set max noise-level parameter (e.g., 10 pixels for point-correspondence problems), replacing a hard inlier threshold with one soft upper bound.

sigma-consensus++ (the IRLS model-polishing step) can be applied both to fit a model from a non-minimal sample AND as a post-processing/refinement step on top of the output of ANY other robust estimator -- directly usable as a refinement layer independent of which sampler/estimator produced the initial model.

Second contribution: Progressive NAPSAC (P-NAPSAC) sampler, starting from PROSAC-style global sampling for the first point of each minimal sample, then drawing remaining points from a local spatial neighborhood (hypersphere) around that first point, with neighborhood size growing progressively across iterations via a growth function g(t). This blends local (NAPSAC) and global (PROSAC/RANSAC) sampling, avoiding NAPSAC's failure modes: imprecise models from badly-conditioned local-only samples, degenerate minimal samples (e.g. 7-point fundamental matrix needing points from >1 plane), and failure when inliers are not spatially coherent.

Pipeline: (1) Gaussian smoothing, 5x5 kernel, sigma=1, to suppress noise; (2) gradient magnitude/orientation via Sobel Gx,Gy using L1 norm G=|Gx|+|Gy| (chosen over L2 purely for speed), orientation quantized to vertical (|Gx|>=|Gy|) or horizontal; (3) anchor-pixel extraction = pixels that are local maxima of G along the quantized gradient direction, thresholded by T_anchor=8, scanned every SI=2 rows/columns to control anchor density/speed; (4) Enhanced Edge Drawing (EED): a single fused pass that does edge-chaining AND line-fitting simultaneously (classical Edge Drawing/EDLines instead does edge-linking first, then a separate line-fitting pass) — this fusion is the core speed win.

EED's per-step neighbor search is reduced versus classical ED: at a 'diagonal' pixel (where the previous pixel's edge orientation differs from the current pixel's, e.g. vertical->horizontal), original ED must examine 6 candidate next-pixels; EED, by assuming the chain should trace a straight line (Bresenham logic), only examines 2 candidates in the diagonal case and 3 in the non-diagonal case. This is faster AND avoids ED's tendency to draw spurious corner-shaped edges at these transitions.

Discontinuity/gap handling: when gradient orientation changes sharply or gradient magnitude falls to ~0, EED does not terminate the segment — it pushes the discontinuity (pixel + direction) onto a stack D_stack and attempts to jump over the gap along the current line's direction (Bresenham-style skip), trying candidate jump lengths J in {5,7,9} pixels (chosen because the 5x5 Gaussian kernel implies any 1px discontinuity affects a >=5px neighborhood). A jump is only accepted if: (a) the already-drawn segment is longer than J; (b) the landing pixel J-pixels away is inside the image and is a non-weak edge pixel (G>0); (c) EED can continue drawing >=J more pixels from there following the edge direction ('extension pixels'); and (d) those extension pixels are well-aligned with the segment, tested via the local gradient auto-correlation matrix M's eigenvalue ratio lambda1/lambda2 >= T_EigenExt=10 and eigenvector angle to the segment normal <= T_AngleExt=10 degrees. If the jump fails, EED instead continues in the NEW (changed) gradient direction and closes/starts a new line-fit — this lets a single anchor point yield multiple separate segments (explicitly shown to raise recall vs EDLines).

Segment validation (post-hoc, cheap): rather than trusting all raw EED segment candidates (many are spurious, esp. in high-edge-density regions like foliage), each candidate is validated by comparing each pixel's gradient orientation to the direction normal to the fitted segment (the 'angular error'), discarding pixels lying inside a jumped discontinuity or near the endpoints first. A segment is accepted if at least 50% of its pixels have angular error below a learned threshold T_valid=0.15 radians (about 8.6 degrees) — chosen to separate a positive-detection angular-error PDF from a negative/random-noise-image angular-error PDF while keeping high recall.

Ablation (Table 3, on YorkUrban, 1-to-1 matching protocol): baseline with NO jumps and NO segment validation: P=0.271, R=0.609, IoU=0.646, F=0.360, bAP=43.06, time=2.95ms. Adding segment validation only: P=0.307, R=0.590, IoU=0.651, F=0.388, bAP=44.27, time=4.16ms. Adding fixed 5px jumps (no jump validation): P=0.278, R=0.702 (big recall jump), IoU=0.712, F=0.383, bAP=42.46, time=3.25ms — but causes bad over-jumps past true endpoints. Adding jump-destination eigen-validation on top of fixed 5px jump: P=0.282, R=0.667, IoU=0.694, F=0.381, bAP=43.62, time=3.48ms. Multi-length jumps {5,7,9}px + jump validation (no segment validation): P=0.285, R=0.686, IoU=0.706, F=0.387, bAP=43.28, time=4.16ms. Full method (multi-length jumps + jump validation + segment validation) = best: P=0.320, R=0.664, IoU=0.711, F=0.415, bAP=44.94, time=5.38ms.

Evaluation protocol (their own contribution): 1-to-1 bipartite matching between detected and ground-truth segments via the Hungarian algorithm on a cost matrix built from a 'structural distance' (perpendicular distance + misalignment + overlap trade-off), gated by IoU>=lambda_overlap=0.1, angular distance<=15 degrees, perpendicular distance<=2*sqrt(2) px — argued to be faster to compute and fairer/more stable than prior point-sampling-based protocols (e.g. MCMLSD's).

Implementation: C++ with Python bindings (pyelsed, pip-installable via 'pip install git+https://github.com/iago-suarez/ELSED.git', requires OpenCV dev headers); designed to avoid floating-point ops where possible and minimize complexity to be CPU-friendly on resource-limited hardware (drones, IoT, phones) where GPU is unavailable or battery-prohibitive to keep active.

Classical/no-training-needed: explicitly a non-learning-based method, contrasted against deep wireframe parsers (AFM, L-CNN, HAWP, HT-HAWP, F-Clip, LERT, SOLD2) which require datasets like the 5,000+-image ShanghaiTech Wireframe corpus; ELSED needs zero labeled training images.

Paper: 'Accurate Tennis Court Line Detection on Amateur Recorded Matches', Sameer Agrawal, Ragoth Sundararajan, Vishak Sagar (NicheSolv India Private Limited, Bengaluru), arXiv:2404.06977, submitted April 10 2024, accepted to 5th Int'l Conf. on Image, Video Processing and AI.

Baseline algorithm improved upon (Farin, Han & de With 2005, 'Fast camera calibration for the analysis of sport sequences', ICME 2005): (1) fixed pixel-threshold filtration to isolate bright/white court-line pixels (paper reuses threshold=180 for comparison), (2) classical Hough Line Detection on the filtered binary image, (3) compute pairwise intersections of detected lines, (4) estimate a homography/transformation matrix from those intersections to a reference tennis-court diagram. Purely classical geometric pipeline, no learned court keypoint model.

Prior ML approaches noted (not adopted): (a) CNN optimizes the initial homography matrix via gradient descent (Jiang et al. 2020, 'Optimizing Through Learned Errors for Accurate Sports Field Registration', WACV 2020); (b) a CNN generates a segmentation mask of court regions instead of computing homography directly (Arroyave Lopez & Echavarria Echeverri 2021 thesis, Universidad de Antioquia). Paper argues both are limited because training data favors professional/televised matches, hurting amateur-court generalization.

New pipeline stage 1 - Shadow removal: MTMT (Multi-task Mean Teacher, Chen et al., CVPR 2020) produces a shadow-detection mask; that mask plus the original image are fed to ShadowFormer (Guo et al., arXiv:2302.01650, 'Global Context Helps Image Shadow Removal') for final shadow removal, run before any line detection.

New pipeline stage 2 - Net cropping: a YOLOv5 object-detection model detects the tennis net location in the shadow-removed image; the frame is cropped to the near side of the net only, then the detected near-side court is geometrically extended to represent the far side (used specifically for low camera-angle amateur footage where the far side is not visible).

New pipeline stage 3 - Color-based pixel filtering (replaces fixed intensity threshold): (a) sample 1000 random points on the image and record their colors, (b) take the modal/most-frequent color as the 'court color', (c) slide a 7x7 window over every pixel A; if at least 4 pixels in the 7x7 neighborhood match the court color AND pixel A itself does NOT match the court color, mark A as a court-line pixel (white). Rationale: a single global brightness threshold (e.g. 180) that works on one court fails on others with different lighting/surface color.

New pipeline stage 4 - Multi-frame scoring/selection: run detection on several randomly sampled frames from a video (not single-frame in production use), draw each candidate court-line hypothesis onto its frame, count how many drawn pixels coincide with actual bright/white pixels in the original image (the 'score'), and keep the highest-scoring detection across the sampled frames as the final court estimate.

Evaluation ground truth / correctness criterion: manual ground-truth court lines were drawn per video; a candidate detection counted as 'correct' if its score (from the stage-4 scoring function) was within 5% of the ground-truth line score. For the head-to-head comparison table, the method was run per-frame (video-level multi-frame scoring was disabled) 'to draw a more accurate and fair comparison' against the old method.

Pipeline: (1) two separate encoder-decoder networks (modified HRNetV2-w48 backbone, high-res representations preserved, 2x upsampling + skip-connection concatenation, softmax output at half input resolution) detect (a) pitch keypoints as single Gaussian heatmap peaks (2px sigma) and (b) line-extremity points as two-Gaussian-peak heatmaps; a 'boundary channel' is added to give the network more global field context.

Detected primitives are expanded hierarchically into multiple keypoint sets: Kp = line-line intersections (~30 pts), Ke = extended line-line intersections, Kp1 = line-ellipse (center-circle) intersections, Kp2 = ellipse tangent points, Kp3 = 13 additional points along the pitch axis/center circle -- i.e. classical projective-geometry construction turns a sparse set of detected lines/points into a much denser point correspondence set before any pose solve.

Calibration solve is a hierarchical/classical geometric method, NOT a single joint nonlinear PnL bundle adjustment as the title might suggest: homography is computed first (via DLT, 'in a minimalist fashion, without further refinement') to disambiguate which candidate keypoint hypothesis is correct, then RANSAC is run with multiple threshold grids across the different keypoint subsets (Kp, Kp1, Kp2 combos), and a 'heuristic voting process' picks the final calibration by lowest reprojection error.

A grid-search over Kp1/Kp2 candidate combinations is used when too few basic keypoints are detected in-frame; a 'Left-Right Disambiguation' heuristic remaps angle-based ambiguity when the camera views along the field's longitudinal axis.

Only a homography-level Levenberg-Marquardt refinement is applied, restricted to ground-plane keypoints -- full 3D camera pose parameters (line correspondences) are used for the hypothesis generation/RANSAC step but not as a joint LM cost term over lines+points as later claimed in title framing; paper text extraction did not surface an explicit combined line+point nonlinear reprojection cost function despite the paper's framing.

Detector training: Adam optimizer (beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999), L2 heatmap regression loss, 200 epochs; Multi-view (MV) variant lr=1e-2 batch=2, Single-view (SV) variant lr=1e-5 batch=2; augmentation = horizontal flip, color jitter, Gaussian noise.

Trained on SoccerNet-Calibration dataset (22,816 images), split SN23-train; two model variants trained -- MV (broad camera-angle coverage) and SV (mostly central/main-broadcast camera shots) -- then each is separately fine-tuned on WorldCup2014 (WC14) and TS-WorldCup (TSWC) target datasets for best per-dataset performance.

Method explicitly stated to be effective only when frame distortion is not severe (fisheye shots called out as an exclusion case) and when a minimum of four keypoints are visible in-frame; frames without enough visible keypoints are marked invalid for calibration rather than calibrated with degraded confidence.

read_facts: pb.vision requires a fixed, elevated camera position: 'at least 5 feet off the ground using a tripod, fence mount, or stable platform' — i.e. mounted, not handheld.

Explicit instruction: 'Avoid handheld footage. The camera must remain steady for consistent tracking' — pb.vision states outright that its tracking pipeline depends on camera steadiness, not on robust motion compensation.
Explicit instruction to DISABLE in-phone image stabilization and HDR before recording ('Make sure to disable image stabilization and HDR').

Full-court, all-4-corners-visible framing is mandated as an input precondition: 'The entire court, including all 4 corners, must be clearly visible in the frame. No part of the court should be cut off.'
All players must remain fully visible at all times including during serve — implies a wide static framing rather than a following/zooming camera.

Lens guidance: use widest lens/zoom needed to keep the whole court+players in frame, 'commonly around 0.5x on many phones' (i.e. ultra-wide lens, which increases distortion — a factor pb.vision apparently tolerates via a static-camera assumption rather than a per-frame distortion/tracking model).
Recording spec: landscape only, H.264 codec, .mp4, 16:9 preferred (other ratios 'should work'), min 1080p@30fps, max 30 minutes per file.

Content/workflow rule: record continuous full match from first serve to paddle tap, no trimming dead time between rallies, no warmups/drills — suggests a single static calibration is computed once (or rarely) per uploaded file rather than tracked per frame.

Title: 'Video-based Sequential Bayesian Homography Estimation for Soccer Field Registration'; method name BHITK (Bayesian Homography Inference from Tracked Keypoints); authors Paul J. Claasen, J.P. de Villiers; v1 Nov 17 2023, v2 May 4 2024 on arXiv.
Two-stage cascaded Kalman filter architecture run every frame: Stage 1 = Linear Keypoint Filter (LKF); Stage 2 = Extended Kalman Filter (EKF).

Stage 1 (LKF) state = image-space keypoint positions x^I_t (stacked per detected keypoint j). Input = per-frame measured/detected keypoint positions y^I_t plus an estimated affine transform A_t (computed via OpenCV global motion compensation between consecutive frames, NOT learned). Dynamics model: x^I_t = A_t x^I_{t-1} + w^{I,j}_t (process noise). Output = filtered keypoint estimates x-hat^I_{t|t} with covariance P^I_{t|t}.

Stage 2 (EKF) state = field-template keypoints plus the 3 columns of the homography matrix H stacked as a vector (x^{F,H}_t = [X^{F,1}...X^{F,j}, h1,h2,h3]). Input = the filtered keypoints coming out of stage 1. Measurement model is nonlinear: x^{I,j}_t = norm(H_t X^{F,j}_t) + w^{M,j}_t, hence EKF (linearization) rather than plain KF. Output = homography estimate H-hat_t.

Core dynamics equation for temporal homography propagation: H_t = A_t H_{t-1}, i.e. the homography at frame t is the previous frame's homography composed with an inter-frame affine motion estimate — this is the explicit frame-to-frame dynamics model the paper proposes as the fix for lack of temporal continuity in single-frame homography estimators.

Process noise for the homography-EKF stage modeled as W^H_t ~ N(0, Sigma^H in R^{9x9}); covariance matrices for both stages are estimated empirically from training data using ground-truth homography annotations and measured keypoint positions (i.e., they are fit per-dataset, not hand-tuned or transferred).

The framework is stated as adaptable/pluggable: it 'can extend existing keypoint detection methods' — meaning any per-frame keypoint/homography detector can be wrapped by LKF+EKF as a temporal smoothing/estimation layer without retraining that detector.

Paper introduces its own annotation tool (KeypointAnnotator, released on GitHub) and a new dataset CARWC (Consolidated and Refined WorldCup) which relabels WC14/TS-WorldCup with denser 'grass band keypoints' (147 of them) replacing the original sparse line-intersection annotations, specifically to support this covariance-fitting approach.

Four-stage pipeline: (1) soccer field modelling generating 5 hierarchical keypoint sets (line-line intersections Kp, extended-line intersections Ke, line-ellipse intersections Kp1, ellipse tangent points Kp2, extra strategic points Kp3) with disambiguation via reprojection-error minimization or grid-search plus heuristic left-right angle thresholding; (2) keypoint/line detection via two encoder-decoder CNNs on modified HRNetV2-w48 backbones producing Gaussian heatmaps (sigma=2px), keypoints extracted by max-pooling; (3) camera parameter estimation using standard pinhole model P=KR[I|-c] with zero skew, known pixel aspect ratio, principal point at image center, NO distortion modeled, calibrated on multiple keypoint subsets (full/main/ground-plane) via a RANSAC grid over several reprojection-error thresholds with heuristic voting; (4) homography estimation via DLT+RANSAC on ground-plane keypoints only, refined with Levenberg-Marquardt to minimize reprojection error.
The method explicitly does NOT model lens distortion (assumes it away) -- a real gap for our wide-angle phone lenses.

No temporal modeling exists in the method as published; authors explicitly state as future work: 'we plan to enhance our approach by incorporating temporal consistency between subsequent video frames, aligning better with the nature of sports video broadcasts.' This directly confirms per-frame-only registration is the state of practice in this line of work as of 2024.

Training requires per-dataset fine-tuning (SoccerNet-trained model fine-tuned separately on WC14 and on TS-WorldCup) to reach best homography numbers -- no true zero-shot cross-venue transfer is demonstrated; the paper reports 'no fine-tune' numbers that are close-to-SOTA but consistently 1-2 IoU points below top methods, and 'fine-tuned' numbers that then match/exceed them.
Best 3D camera-parameter results are achieved WITHOUT iterative bundle-adjustment-style refinement -- just DLT+RANSAC -- the paper's headline claim ('no bells, just whistles').
Data augmentation used: random horizontal flip, color jitter, Gaussian noise -- no synthetic-rendering augmentation pipeline described.

Code released at https://github.com/mguti97/No-Bells-Just-Whistles; the WebFetch pass found no explicit license statement in the paper text and no mention of released model weights (only code repo referenced).

Core idea: for each new frame, instead of starting inverse-compositional KLT optimization at the previous frame's warp p_t, start at a gyro-predicted warp p0_{t+1} computed by integrating gyroscope angular rate over the inter-frame interval into a rotation R_cam, forming a 2D homography H = K R_cam K^-1 (K = camera intrinsics), and using H to predict the affine warp (A_pred, b_pred) via forward composition (Eqs. 15-21).

Tracking motion model: an 8-DOF affine-photometric warp (6 affine params a1..a6 for A,b + scale alpha + offset beta for illumination), from Jin et al. 2001, instead of a simple 2-DOF translation model; chosen specifically to handle camera rolling (in-plane rotation) and outdoor illumination change.

Uses inverse-compositional Lucas-Kanade (fixed Hessian computed once from template gradient, not recomputed each iteration) for efficiency; Hessian inversion is O(n^3) in warp-parameter count n (n=8 for affine vs n=2 translation, i.e. 64x more compute), motivating GPU acceleration.

Multi-resolution image pyramid (3-5 levels) used for larger motion tolerance; template re-registered as a new template only when tracking-quality checks fail: (i) sum of squared error, (ii) normalized cross-correlation, (iii) template shearing (area ratio) exceed thresholds -- otherwise template A/b/alpha/beta persist across frames rather than resetting every frame.

Gyroscope model: cluster of m>=3 single-axis MEMS gyros, raw measurement z = S^T*omega + v + eta (S = 3xm shape/scale-alignment matrix, v = bias vector, eta = Gaussian noise). Camera/gyro fusion homography requires camera intrinsics K, gyro shape/bias (S,v), and relative rotation R_ic between camera and gyro frames.

Auto-calibration procedure needs NO checkerboard: uses only KLT feature-track homographies from natural landmarks under rotation-dominant camera motion. Steps: (1) camera self-calibration from n homographies via Hartley 1997 linear method (Eq.24, Cholesky) + nonlinear reprojection-error refinement; (2) gyroscope shape S via factorization method (SVD-based, Hwangbo & Kanade 2008); (3) relative orientation R_ic via SVD/Arun 1987 absolute orientation between rotation-axis bundles; (4) 'total refinement' - joint nonlinear optimization of all 17 params (K:5, S:6, v:3, R_ic:3) minimizing total reprojection error across raw gyro integration and feature correspondences (Eq. 27).

Key calibration accuracy driver is the distance ratio rho = d/||t|| (scene depth / camera translation magnitude during rotation) -- self-calibration from pure-rotation homographies degrades as camera translation increases relative to scene distance; RANSAC-based homography estimation is used to reject outlier correspondences before calibration.

Time synchronization between gyro and camera streams (no hardware trigger) is solved by finding a scalar time offset t_o that maximizes normalized cross-correlation between optical-flow-derived rotation rate and gyro angular rate magnitude (phase-lag method), since commodity sensors lack precise sync.
