read_facts: Monte Carlo simulation (25,000 runs per bounce position, 20 positions up to 100mm from line) models each observer's (Hawk-Eye-referenced) true ball position perturbed by an independent zero-mean Gaussian uncertainty distribution to predict probability of 'in'/'out' misjudgment and probability of a challenge being made.

Best-fit Gaussian 'space constant' (sigma) parameters differ by observer type and line type: line judges 22mm overall (32mm base/service, 14mm side/centre); players 30mm overall (46mm base/service, 26mm side/centre) - i.e., a per-line-type, per-observer-type uncertainty budget rather than a single global number.

Ground truth for 'true' ball bounce position was Hawk-Eye's multi-camera triangulated 3D trajectory reconstruction, claimed accurate to 3mm; challenge/judge data were binned into 5mm bins relative to the line for analysis.
Line judges positioned 5.5m from base/service lines and 8.7m from side/centre lines; greater viewing distance associated with larger uncertainty (wider sigma) for base/service line calls.

Model separately fits psychometric functions (midpoint/P50, slope=space constant) per line-type category (base/service vs side/centre) rather than one global curve, since underlying uncertainty differs geometrically by court region.

The framework treats real-world challenge-system records as psychophysical response data: physical stimulus = Hawk-Eye ball position relative to line; response = binary in/out call or challenge decision, enabling logistic/Gaussian psychometric fitting without a controlled lab experiment.
Core linearized error-model formula: for y = f(x) with x ~ N(mu_x, Sigma_x), y approx f(mu_x) + J(x-mu_x), so Sigma_y approx J*Sigma_x*J^T (first-order/delta-method covariance propagation), Eq.(1).

For partitioned inputs x=(a,b): Sigma_y approx [J_a J_b] * [[Sigma_a, Sigma_ab],[Sigba, Sigma_b]] * [J_a; J_b]^T, allowing cross-covariance between e.g. a homography H and a point x to be propagated jointly (Eq. 1 generalization).

Novel contribution: closed-form analytical Jacobian for ANY projective bilinear mapping C = AB/||AB|| (normalized product form), applicable uniformly to point-camera projection (x~PX), homography composition (H_ac ~ H_bc H_ab), and point-to-line mapping under fundamental matrix (l' ~ Fx) -- obviating need for mapping-specific Jacobian derivations (previously homography Jacobian [Criminisi 1999] and fundamental-matrix Jacobian [Csurka et al. 1997; Zhang 1998] had to be derived separately).

Jacobians: J_a = dc/da = (1/||AB||) * [(I_{mxm} kron B^T) - c*vec(BC^T)^T]  (Eq.2); J_b = dc/db = (1/||AB||) * [(A kron I_{nxn}) - c*vec(C^T A)^T] (Eq.3). Kronecker-product based, derived in appendix via product/norm quotient rule on vec(AB).

Search-region / guided-matching bound: for Gaussian x with mean mu_x and covariance Sigma_x, squared Mahalanobis distance (x-mu_x)^T Sigma_x^+ (x-mu_x) <= k^2 contains fraction alpha of instances, where k^2 = inverse CDF of chi-square with r=rank(Sigma_x) degrees of freedom at probability alpha (Eq.4) -- this is the standard basis for confidence ellipses/ellipsoids used to bound a feature search region.

Because homogeneous 2D points/lines have rank-2 (not rank-3) covariance, the paper derives via duality that the set of equal-likelihood points forms a conic C = [mu_x mu_x^T - k^2 Sigma_x]^{-1} (Eq.7, an ellipse containing mu_x), and dually for lines the bounding conic is a hyperbola C = mu_l mu_l^T - k^2 Sigma_l (Eq.8) -- point-in-ellipse test: x0 is inside iff x0^T C x0 has same sign as mu_x^T C mu_x.

Generalizes to n dimensions: homogeneous n-D point X bounded by quadric Q = [mu_X mu_X^T - k^2 Sigma_X]^{-1} (ellipsoid), dual hyperplane pi bounded by Q* similarly -- directly generalizes to 3D world-point uncertainty ellipsoids, relevant to lifting a court corner tap (2D image point with covariance) through a homography to a 3D/metric world point with a resulting uncertainty ellipse/ellipsoid.

Experimental pipeline (Sec 4): maximum-likelihood homography/fundamental-matrix estimate and ITS covariance obtained via: (1) Forstner operator for interest-point detection in both images; (2) initial correspondence via highest local normalized cross-correlation; (3) RANSAC for linear model + inlier set; (4) reprojection error minimized via sparse Levenberg-Marquardt (two-view bundle adjustment / 2D block adjustment); (5) covariance matrix of estimated parameters retrieved from the LM solve (inverse of the approximate Hessian/normal-equations matrix, standard bundle-adjustment covariance extraction).

Homography H (as a 3x3 or 2x2 planar collineation, framed here as a linear system A·h=0 from point correspondences) is estimated by finding the eigenvector v of A^T A associated with its smallest eigenvalue λ (the standard DLT/least-squares homography solution).

Covariance of that eigenvector-estimated homography parameter vector is obtained via matrix perturbation theory applied around the zero-residual (noise-free) solution, not via a closed-form inverse of the normal equations.

Closed-form covariance: Σ_v = Σ_i [λ_i / (λ - λ_i)^2] · (a_i a_i^T), summed over the other eigenpairs (λ_i, v_i) of A^T A, where a_i are rows of the design matrix A (excluding the i=that-of-λ term, i.e. it's a sum over all eigenvalues other than the smallest one being estimated).

This derivation deliberately avoids ever inverting a matrix, which the authors state keeps it 'well conditioned' even when A^T A is singular or near-singular (e.g. the noise-free case, or the minimal n=4 point-correspondence case where A^T A can be exactly singular).

Input requirements: n point correspondences between an image plane and a world (reference) plane, n >= 4 (4 is the minimal case for a homography); noise is modeled as a homogeneous, isotropic Gaussian process on the measured coordinates.

Two noise variance parameters appear in the surrounding derivation: σ_I^2 for image-point measurement noise and σ_W^2 for world-point (reference plane) measurement noise, i.e. the framework supports uncertain correspondences on both sides, not just the image side.

In the companion page (node4), the same document frames a related first/second-order error-propagation analysis for a 'plane measuring device,' decomposing a general homography H as H = A_aff · P, where A_aff is an affine part and P is the purely-perspective (non-linear) part; when P = identity (i.e., no perspective distortion), first-order linear error propagation is exact and no second-order correction is needed.

The residual/perturbation approach assumes the true residual error goes to zero as measurement noise goes to zero (i.e., the eigenvector-based covariance is a local linearization around the noiseless solution), and the document states no explicit numerical validity threshold is given for when this linearization breaks down.

Pipeline is fully monocular, 2D-only: (1) per-frame ball positioning via two-stage method combining background subtraction (MOG Gaussian mixture / KNN) + color area filtering; (2) fit two least-squares curves to the trajectory (falling trend, rising trend); (3) compute intersection of the two curves as the predicted bouncing point; (4) determine in/out by comparing the 2D image-coordinate bouncing point directly against manually marked court side-lines. No 3D reconstruction, no camera calibration, no court measurement/virtual court model at all.

Explicitly contrasted with binocular Hawk-Eye pipeline, which needs: multiple high-speed calibrated cameras, court measurement + virtual 3D court reconstruction, ball detection per camera view, triangulation to 3D world coords from >=2 simultaneous camera views, 3D trajectory fit, then mapping bounce to the reconstructed court model. Their method skips all of this and works purely in image-plane 2D relative position.

Bounce-point prediction targets a specific failure mode: near the bounce, the trajectory's slope change is not obvious (due to ball speed, spin, and camera viewing angle), making it ambiguous which frames are 'descending' vs 'ascending'. Fix: mark ambiguous frames as 'uncertain points', enumerate assignments of each uncertain point to the descending or ascending set, refit both least-squares curves per combination, and select the assignment/combination that minimizes total fitting MSE (this is the 'minimum fitting loss of uncertain point' method). The intersection of the winning pair of fitted curves is the final bounce point.
Used an existing open-source least-squares curve-fitting library (lmfit-py, github.com/lmfit/lmfit-py) rather than a custom fitting implementation.

In/out ground-truth judging was done manually by a human comparing the predicted bounce point's image position to manually marked court lines -- there is no automated court-line detection component in the system at all; court lines are marked, not detected.

Capture setup: a single fixed (non-panning/non-tracking) Samsung S10 phone camera in slow-motion mode (240 fps), positioned to cover the service line and baseline on one side of the court -- i.e., a static, single, non-moving camera, unlike our moving-camera setup.

Paper explicitly names camera-position/viewpoint and camera-to-line distance as the two dominant error sources: 'the main reason is that there is strong interference and the court line is far away from the camera' for the representative misjudged sample, and a follow-on qualitative test (Fig 6a-c) shows bounces in the middle of the court are easy to judge but bounces near the baseline or far sideline (farther from / more oblique to the camera) are difficult even for a human reviewing the video multiple times, because the monocular method relies on 2D relative position rather than 3D reconstruction, so different camera positions cause parallax that directly degrades accuracy.

Authors' own proposed remediation (future work, not implemented): use four cameras per court (two per side: one between the service line and baseline, one at the mid-sideline) to reduce the parallax/distance problem inherent to a single monocular view -- i.e., even this monocular-vision paper concludes multi-camera coverage is needed to fix the near-line/far-line failure mode, they did not solve it monocularly.

read_facts: Stage 1 - geometric-properties-driven keypoint generation from soccer-field geometry: hierarchical keypoint sets Kp (up to 30 line-line intersections from boundary/penalty markings), Kpe (extended non-adjacent line-line intersections within 50% of image boundary), Kp1 (line-ellipse intersections derived analytically for circles/semi-circles), Kp2 (ellipse tangent points from external points), Kp3 (9 extra points along central axis + quarter-turn markers); disambiguation via reprojection-error minimization + cross-product consistency checks.

Stage 2 - a LEARNED keypoint/line-extremity heatmap network (modified HRNetV2-w48 encoder, 2x upsampling + skip connections) trained with L2 heatmap loss on SoccerNet-Calibration, separate softmax head for keypoints and sigmoid head for line extremities. PnLCalib is NOT purely classical/geometric end-to-end -- it still depends on a trained CNN detector for keypoints/lines; only the camera-parameter solve (stages 3-4) is classical.

Stage 3 - closed-form Direct Linear Transformation (DLT) for camera parameters, with RANSAC (grid-searched reprojection-error thresholds) over multiple heuristic keypoint subsets (full/main/ground-plane); intrinsics pre-estimated if >=6 keypoints available, else joint K/R/t optimization.

Stage 4 (paper's core contribution) - Levenberg-Marquardt 'Points-n-Lines' (PnL) refinement minimizing combined cost C = alpha*sum(line-to-line distances, both extremities) + (1-alpha)*sum(point distances); alpha=0.6 fixed empirically. Includes explicit correction (Eq. 4) for keypoints projecting behind the camera plane during optimization.

Two model variants: OursMV (multi-view, trained 200 epochs on full SN23-train, lr 1e-2, batch 2, Adam) and OursSV (single-view, trained 200 epochs on non-replay/central-camera SN23 subset, lr 1e-5, batch 1), each then fine-tuned on WC14/TSWC with non-planar points masked out during fine-tuning.

Keypoint/line ground truth for training is DERIVED from ground-truth homographies projected onto the known field-plane template (SoccerNet's 23-line segment definitions), not manually re-annotated per new venue -- i.e. the field TEMPLATE/geometry is assumed known and fixed (a single soccer pitch layout), unlike our multi-venue pickleball problem.

Explicitly documented failure modes: 'close-up shots with few/no landmarks' and 'fisheye shots from inside goals with extreme lens distortion' cause large accuracy drops going from the curated SN22-test-center subset to the full SN23-test set (79.5% FS -> 61.8% FS, a 17.7-point drop), attributed to the standard perspective camera model's inability to handle extreme/fisheye viewpoints.

Inference cost: baseline single RANSAC-config/single keypoint-subset run is 164ms; full heuristic voting (6 RANSAC configs x 3 keypoint subsets) is 439ms; adding the PnL refinement module adds only ~8% overhead (164ms -> 178ms) on top of the cheap baseline path.

SAM2 (Segment Anything Model 2, Meta/FAIR) is a promptable visual segmentation model for both images and videos, using a streaming memory architecture (memory encoder + memory bank + memory attention) to propagate a mask prediction across video frames given a point/box/mask prompt on one frame.
Repository LICENSE file (facebookresearch/sam2, root LICENSE) is the Apache License 2.0 full text.

Repo states explicitly: 'The SAM 2 model checkpoints, SAM 2 demo code (front-end and back-end), and SAM 2 training code are licensed under Apache 2.0' -- i.e. code AND released checkpoints/weights are covered by the same Apache-2.0 grant, not a separate restrictive weights license.

Apache-2.0 license grants a perpetual, worldwide, non-exclusive, no-charge, royalty-free, irrevocable copyright license to reproduce, prepare derivative works, publicly display/perform, sublicense, and distribute the work and derivative works, in source or object form.
Obligations: retain copyright/attribution notices, mark modified files with prominent notice of changes; work provided AS IS with no warranty (standard Apache-2.0 disclaimer).

Some third-party components in the repo (e.g. a cc_torch custom CUDA op) carry a separate BSD-3-Clause license -- so the repo is not 100% single-licensed, but the core model/checkpoints/demo/training code is Apache-2.0.
No non-commercial (NC) restriction anywhere in the stated licensing -- contrasts with many research-model releases (e.g. some SAM-adjacent or dataset licenses) that use CC-BY-NC.

Baseline ('Old Method', from cited Farin/Han/de With 2005 fast-camera-calibration work): pixel-brightness threshold filter (paper uses threshold=180) to isolate white/bright pixels -> Hough Line Detection on filtered image -> compute pairwise line intersections -> estimate transformation/homography matrix mapping intersections to a reference court diagram.

New pipeline stage order per frame: (1) MTMT model generates a shadow-mask from the raw image; (2) shadow-mask + original image both fed to ShadowFormer for final shadow removal, producing a shadow-cleaned image; (3) YOLO-v5 runs object detection for the tennis net on the shadow-removed image; net coordinates are used to crop the frame to only the near-side court (needed because a low camera angle makes the far side hard to see/parse); the detected near-side court is then geometrically extended to reconstruct the full court; (4) the new court-color-based pixel filtration (1000 random sample points -> modal color -> 7x7-neighborhood-based line classification) replaces the old global brightness threshold; (5) Hough Line Detection + intersection + homography estimation proceeds as in the old method on this filtered/cropped image; (6) for video (not single-image) input, run detection on multiple randomly sampled frames and apply the scoring algorithm (draw candidate lines onto frame, count overlap with bright pixels in original image) to select the best-scoring/most-accurate court detection across frames.

Motivating failure modes explicitly identified for amateur/consumer footage (Figure 1): low camera angle hiding the far court side; court partially occluded by foreground objects/players; scuffed/dirty/faded court lines that are not clearly bright/white.

Old pixel-filtration method's core weakness (Figure 4 example): the single global brightness threshold that works for one court (e.g., threshold 180) is wrong for another court, either missing lines or leaving residual white noise elsewhere on court -- motivating the switch to a per-image adaptive court-color background model instead of a fixed global threshold.

For fair, isolated method comparison the authors deliberately ran their method on individual still frames only (not full video + scoring pipeline) when producing the Table 1 head-to-head vs. the Old Method, to isolate the filtration/shadow-removal/cropping gains from the video-level scoring-selection gains.

Hawk-Eye is a computer vision system that triangulates ball position/trajectory using visual images and timing data from multiple high-speed cameras at different fixed, pre-surveyed locations/angles around the field of play (fixed multi-camera rig, not a single moving monocular camera).
Tennis deployment uses 10 cameras; general deployments use up to 10 high-performance cameras, typically mounted on the underside of the stadium roof.

Pipeline stages: (1) identify pixels corresponding to the ball in each camera view, (2) compute 3D ball position by comparing at least two physically separate camera views at the same instant (multi-view triangulation), (3) assemble the succession of triangulated positions into a trajectory, (4) predict/extrapolate the future ball path, (5) interpret the trajectory against a predefined geometric model of the playing area/court lines plus rules-of-game logic to render in/out or scoring decisions.

Backend maintains a 'data store' containing a predefined model of the playing-area geometry plus rules-of-the-game logic used to interpret ball trajectory against lines/zones — i.e. court geometry is a fixed, pre-loaded model, not solved per-frame from a moving camera.
System outputs are near-real-time graphics for umpires/officials, broadcast TV, and coaching staff.

Originally developed in 2000 by engineers at Roke Manor Research Limited (a Siemens subsidiary), Romsey, England; inventors Paul Hawkins and David Sherry filed then withdrew a UK patent application — core method IP protection is unclear/limited per this source.

Company was commercially acquired: Wisden Group (14 June 2006, £4.4m), then Sony (March 2011, £15m-£20m); currently a Sony subsidiary — fully proprietary, closed commercial system; no open code or weights are published.
Deployed in 20+ sports: baseball, cricket, tennis, badminton, hurling, rugby union, soccer, Gaelic football, American football, volleyball, snooker, Australian football.
